title: Demand & Elasticity
description: Price elasticity, total revenue, demand curves

questions.statement: If a good is highly inelastic, a price increase will cause a sharp decrease in total revenue.
questions.payload: With inelastic demand, buyers keep purchasing even at higher prices, so revenue increases.

questions.statement: Luxury goods tend to have elastic demand.
questions.payload: Correct. Consumers can easily forgo luxuries when prices rise, making demand sensitive to price changes.

questions.statement: A price elasticity of demand of 0.3 indicates elastic demand.
questions.payload: A PED below 1 is inelastic. Elastic demand requires a PED greater than 1.

questions.statement: Goods with many close substitutes tend to be more price elastic.
questions.payload: Correct. If a substitute is available, consumers will switch when price rises — making demand more responsive.

questions.statement: When demand is perfectly inelastic, the demand curve is horizontal.
questions.payload: Perfectly inelastic demand is a vertical line — quantity doesn't change regardless of price.

questions.statement: A necessity like insulin has highly elastic demand.
questions.payload: Necessities have inelastic demand — consumers must buy them regardless of price changes.

questions.statement: If PED = 1, a price rise will leave total revenue unchanged.
questions.payload: Correct. Unit elastic demand means the percentage fall in quantity exactly cancels out the percentage price rise.

questions.statement: Demand tends to become more elastic over a longer time period.
questions.payload: Correct. Over time, consumers find more substitutes and change habits, making demand more responsive to price changes.

questions.statement: An inferior good has a positive income elasticity of demand.
questions.payload: Inferior goods have a negative income elasticity — demand falls as income rises.

questions.statement: A rise in the price of a complementary good will increase demand for the original good.
questions.payload: Complements are consumed together. If the complement's price rises, its demand falls — pulling down demand for the original good too.

questions.statement: Cross price elasticity of demand between substitutes is positive.
questions.payload: Correct. When two goods are substitutes, a price rise in one increases demand for the other — positive XED.

questions.statement: Advertising primarily affects the price elasticity of demand.
questions.payload: Advertising shifts the demand curve rightward — it increases demand, not primarily its price sensitivity.

questions.statement: When a firm lowers price and total revenue falls, demand is price elastic.
questions.payload: If price falls and revenue also falls, demand is inelastic — the quantity increase doesn't compensate for the price cut.

questions.statement: A normal good has a positive income elasticity of demand.
questions.payload: Correct. As income rises, demand for normal goods increases — making YED positive.

questions.statement: The demand for petrol in the short run is relatively inelastic.
questions.payload: Correct. In the short run, drivers have few alternatives to petrol, making demand unresponsive to price changes.

questions.statement: A perfectly elastic demand curve indicates consumers will buy any quantity at the current price, but nothing above it.
questions.payload: Correct. Perfectly elastic demand is a horizontal line — infinite response to even the smallest price change.
